context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m stuck on the city stressors table tbh, idk how detailed the ratings/explanations are supposed to be. can u help me figure out what counts as “enough”?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to be thinking about! Before I weigh in — what have you written so far for one of your stressors? Share an example rating and explanation you've drafted, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable question about the depth of justification required. Rather than telling them what 'enough' looks like, I should turn the question back to them to activate their own thinking. I'll ask them to reflect on what they've already written or attempted, so I can assess where they actually are and guide from there. This keeps the Socratic approach and avoids giving a prescriptive answer.

exchanges.student: i barely have anything rn tbh, just random stuff like heat + flooding. that angle didn’t really help lol—can u just say what a solid explanation usually looks like?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you a template — but you don't need one yet! You already have two stressors: heat and flooding. Pick one and tell me: why does that stressor matter for your city specifically? What's actually happening there because of it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a template or model answer, which I should not do. Instead, I should redirect them to start working with what they have. They've identified two stressors (heat and flooding), which is a starting point. I'll push them to begin engaging with the assignment using those examples rather than waiting for a formula. Keeping it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: ngl that’s kinda my problem, i don’t know what “specific” means here. like do they want a whole paragraph per stressor or just 1-2 lines w/ a reason?
exchanges.tutor: Re-read Step 4 of the assignment: it asks for evidence or conditions that support your rating. That should tell you something about what's expected. But honestly, format concerns are secondary right now — which city did you choose? Let's start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same anxiety about format/length rather than engaging with the content. This is a distraction from actually doing the work. I should redirect them firmly but kindly — the assignment itself gives clear guidance in Step 4, and the real issue is that they haven't started researching their city yet. I'll point them back to the assignment's own language and push them toward content rather than format.

exchanges.student: i picked jakarta, but now i’m overthinking all of it fr. quick track note: format-push didn’t work, so different angle—can u just tell me if “frequent flooding, worsened by sea level rise and land subsidence” is too vague?
exchanges.tutor: That's a real start — good. Now ask yourself: if someone had never heard of Jakarta, would that sentence tell them *how bad* the flooding actually is there? What specific evidence about Jakarta could you add to make that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student shared an actual attempt at a stressor description, which is progress. I should acknowledge this and engage with it Socratically rather than just validating or dismissing it. The description is a decent start but lacks the 'evidence or conditions' that Step 4 requires. I should ask them a question that pushes them to think about what's missing — specifically the 'why' and 'how severe' for Jakarta — without telling them it's wrong or right.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe “it happens a lot and affects a lot of people”? i’m still not sure what level of detail they want, like am i supposed to research stats for every single one?
exchanges.tutor: 'It happens a lot and affects a lot of people' isn't really evidence — that could describe any city. Jakarta has some pretty striking documented facts about its flooding and subsidence. What do you actually know about what's happening there? Have you looked it up yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but still generalizing and returning to the format/detail anxiety. The phrase 'it happens a lot and affects a lot of people' is too vague and I should be direct about that without giving the answer. I should push them toward concrete thinking about Jakarta specifically — Jakarta is a well-documented case with lots of data on flooding and subsidence, so I should encourage them to look it up rather than guess. Being slightly critical here is appropriate.

exchanges.student: not much tbh, just that parts are sinking. maybe i should switch cities lol, but before that—if i *don’t* have stats yet, is a rating like 9 basically impossible to justify?
exchanges.tutor: Don't switch cities — Jakarta is a great case study and you already know something about it. As for the rating: don't worry about justifying a 9 in the abstract. Go look up Jakarta's flooding and subsidence situation first, then come back. What you find will answer your question better than I can.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now suggesting switching cities as an avoidance tactic, and also asking a genuine question about whether stats are required for high ratings. I should firmly discourage the city switch as unnecessary and redirect them. On the stats question, I should push back Socratically — they don't need to answer the format question before doing the research. The real issue is they haven't researched Jakarta yet. I should be direct: go look it up.

exchanges.student: ugh okay, but i’m trying not to spiral rn. different angle: can u at least tell me the minimum that would make a 9 feel believable for flooding in jakarta?
exchanges.tutor: I hear that you don't want to spiral — but asking me for the minimum is itself part of the spiral. The only way out is to just go look up Jakarta flooding. Spend 10 minutes on it and come back with one thing you found. That'll move you forward faster than anything I can say.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to give them a formula or minimum threshold before doing the research. This is the third or fourth time they've circled around the same anxiety. I should be firm — I won't give them a minimum standard, and I should redirect them clearly to just start the research. A little tough love is appropriate here. The student needs to break the spiral by acting, not by getting more reassurance.

exchanges.student: fair, but i’m still blanking lol. can u give me literally one example of the kind of evidence line i should be trying to find?
exchanges.tutor: Notice we've been going in circles on this for a while. I'm not going to give you an example line — that's your job. Open a browser, search 'Jakarta flooding subsidence,' and tell me what you find. Literally anything you come back with is better than another round of this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to model the answer for them. I've been firm multiple times and they keep circling. This is now a clear redundancy pattern — same request, different framing, multiple times. I should refuse to give an example and instead redirect them very concretely to just start searching, giving them a nudge toward action rather than more discussion. I should also note the pattern directly.

exchanges.student: ok ok, trying a diff angle since that didn’t work: i found “north jakarta sinks faster in some areas,” but idk how to turn that into the table entry.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you found something real. Now think about it: if parts of North Jakarta are sinking, what does that mean for the people and infrastructure there when flooding happens? What's the consequence of sinking land meeting rising water?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student finally did some research and came back with a finding. This is genuine progress and I should acknowledge it positively. Now I can be Socratic and help them think through how to connect that finding to the assignment's requirements (Step 4: evidence supporting a severity rating). I should ask them what that fact implies for Jakarta's vulnerability, pushing them to make the analytical connection themselves.

exchanges.student: it prob means flooding gets worse and drainage/infrastructure struggle more, but i still can’t tell if that’s enough for the justification.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction. Now stop asking if it's enough and actually write it out — draft your stressor entry for flooding in Jakarta: the category, your rating, and your justification using what you just figured out. Bring it back and we'll look at it together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuine analytical connection now — this is good progress. However, they're still seeking validation rather than committing to their own reasoning. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without confirming it's 'enough,' and push them to now actually write something down and bring it together. We've had enough back-and-forth on this concept — it's time to ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a written attempt.
